author: @Mr31Vince
text:
I thought Id do a dirty and simple test experiment first, as a baseline before trying out any carbon felt or other materials and was quite surprised at my results!
I hammered a rusty steel stake into the ground a few inches and then jubilee clipped a length of copper cable (speaker wire) to it. Then I cut around a 2 m length of 1.5mm twin and earth flat mains cable and stripped out the brown and blue wires so they were separate. I straightened out the brown wire as best I could by hand, exposed about 1cm of copper core at one end and stuck it along a wooden beam that runs  across the inside of my garden shed.  I connected my multimeter to both wires, the one from the ground stake and the other to the end of the suspended mains cable and switched it to AC volts. The meter showed 625 which I thought wasnt bad for a dirty quick test, then I realised it was actually reading 6.25 volts!  I switched it to the freq setting and it read out 50hz.  So to see what happened next I suspended the spare blue wire I also had, but seeing as I had nowhere else to suspend it I just stuck it alongside the brown wire that was already there so they were both sitting next to each other and even touching in places (both wires still have the full insulation on them). I connected both antenna wire ends together so they were in parallel as that was the easiest thing to do. I didnt expect it to do anything really, but the voltage now read 9.35v AC, a 50% improvement.  No bad for a 2 m length of old mains cable.  I made up the circuit you showed with a couple of spare caps I had lying around (400 nf I think) and set it to charge the electrolytic capacitors.  I'll check the max DC volts on the caps tomorrow.

replies.author: @Mr31Vince
replies.text: 1 day later and the electrolytic caps were charged to 57v dc.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome mate - interesting results

author: @johnmorrison6814
text:
Random thought for you.  A piece of conductive metal (zinc) would have a resonant frequency it works perfectly at.  This would be where it collects the most energy.  
Carbon felt would have multiple conductive paths through it and as the material flexes these paths would change.  My random thought is.  The multiple paths would have multiple resonant frequencies and thus would collect energy over multiple frequencies.  Anyway thought I should share that in case it may help you or someone else.

author: @peterdkay
text:
I built up your exact circuit and measured 2V across output however when loaded with a 1 megohm load the voltage dropped to 1V. This meant output power was limited to 1 microwatt. Almost useless!
I put an oscilloscope between aerial and common of output capacitors and measured about 2V peak to peak of 50Hz which is consistant with 2VDC output. This proves that the voltage I measured was generated by stray field from house wiring.

author: @zattan2002
text: Hi mate! Very interesting as usual. May i suggest a few experiments? As you may now, nano-particules have very effective antenna properties. when two particules are close enough, their sensivity is multiplied by 10.000. These particules are very easy to obtain by a chemist. One could make a coating that could be applied on clothes in order to charge phone, for example, but i think that the sensivity would be surprisingly great. Another idea that may seems crazy would be to put a nail in a tree and connect it like an antenna and look what we get. Nano-particles are everywhere. Depending on their size, they resonates with specific frequency. Also, phones antennas uses fractal design in order to minimise size. Think about it... Cheers!

author: @aleksandar7393
text:
Just a question and maybe something to be careful of:
When you were doing your measurement have you used it at the same time with any other antenna?
Reason for this is that measurements can vary simply because of the time you are testing there is increase in RF activity, WiFi router, radio station, TV or what ever else is collecting  - therefore if you measure at specific time without knowing what actual radio wave spectrum you collecting, reading can be misleading. Also worth adding in centimetre wave length small movement in room can mean different result, and you can see that from phone apps that measure field strength in room.

author: @aga5897
text:
With Radio Frequencies there is this thing called 'skin effect' where the energy travels around the Outside of a conductor rather than through the entire bulk of the conductive material.
Graphene sounds like an ideal candidate, or any other 6-member carbon ring for that matter.
The carbons will be sp2 hybridised leaving that final p orbital free to to form a delocalised electron orbital kinda like a doughnut above and below the plane of the ring, which sounds ideal for the 'skin effect'.
Activated carbon isn't Graphene, but maybe there is some graphene character in there.
Pyrolyse a slice of bread and try that as an antenna if you get the time/inclination.
Depending on how long you leave it in the heat you'll end up with just carbon or a highly complex load of aromatics on a carbon framework, of which the aromatic version should work rather well.
